subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Uses ESS terminology correctly (noise pollution, decibels, t-test)
Report follows conventional IA structure with numbered sections
Logical progression of ideas is generally maintained
weaknesses: Formatting glitches and LaTeX artefacts impede flow
Verbosity and casual phrasing reduce concision
Inconsistent p-value notation and sudden topic shifts disturb coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure, terminology, and coherence consistently align with band-2 descriptors: the report is organized and uses appropriate terms but lacks sustained concision and flawless logic, resulting in 2 marks.

strengths: Focused and relevant research question linked to local aircraft noise context
Comprehensive background on noise pollution with credible citations (WHO, National Geographic)
Clear explanation of site-specific factors (distance to KLIA2, measured dB levels)
weaknesses: Research question phrasing is awkward and conflates two variables
Connections between issue and question are described but fragmented
Minor citation inaccuracies reduce academic rigour
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The background discussion meets high-band descriptors, but the research question formulation and linkage to the issue fall within the mid band. On balance, the criterion performance aligns with a score of 4 (midâupper range of the 3â4 band).

strengths: Method outline (online Likert-scale survey, target sample sizes, statistical tests) is repeatable
Stratified random sampling is clearly justified with reference to representativeness
Survey structure table aligns sections with research aims
weaknesses: Full survey instrument and scoring rubric are missing, limiting repeatability
Variable table mislabels dependent and independent variables
Risk and ethical considerations are outlined but lack a systematic assessment of psychological stress and bias
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Sampling justification achieved top-band descriptors, but method design and risk assessment are in the mid band. The overall performance averages to the mid-band level, meriting a score of 3.

strengths: Evaluates method strengths (large sample, ease of survey) and weaknesses (self-report bias, location limitation)
Comments on reliability and external validity in an environmental context
Demonstrates evaluative thinking beyond mere description
weaknesses: Suggestions for improvements are limited to a single workshop idea
Discussion omits socioeconomic and cultural factors affecting awareness
Some causal wording (âinfluenced by genderâ) oversteps the correlation evidence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Conclusion evaluation and method evaluation align with mid-band descriptors; suggested improvements fall in the lower band. Overall, the balance of performance warrants a score of 3.

strengths: Appropriate choice of diagrams and bar charts to present all relevant data
Correct application of statistical analysis (means, SDs, two-sample t-test) with worked formulas
Conclusion logically follows from descriptive statistics and t-test results
weaknesses: Charts and tables contain formatting artefacts and inconsistent labeling
Calculation notation errors (p-value formatting, missing t-test assumptions) undermine clarity
No confidence intervals or effect-size discussion to fully support the conclusion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation, analysis, and conclusions consistently meet the 3â4 band descriptors without reaching completeness or full accuracy. A midpoint score of 3 is appropriate.

strengths: Proposal of an interactive workshop links application to key findings
Identifies some strengths (hands-on engagement) and weaknesses (resource needs, participation) of the solution
weaknesses: Justification of the workshopâs effectiveness is weak and qualitative
Lacks concrete metrics or evaluation plan for assessing impact
Does not explore policy or scalability considerations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both application strands meet the descriptors for band-2 performance: the proposal is described with weak justification and some evaluation of strengths/weaknesses, meriting 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key ESS terms (noise pollution, decibel, standard deviation, t-test) are used correctly; however, verbosity and casual phrasing (âDid you always use it?â) show limited concision. Either appropriate terminology or concision is achieved, not both, so band 2 is appropriate.

The student comments on reliability, error bars and external validity (urban vs rural), showing evaluative thinking, yet the discussion is brief and omits socioeconomic or cultural factors. This is an evaluation with omissions, satisfying band 3-4.

The conclusion (females more aware) follows directly from the descriptive statistics and t-test; data are quoted. However, no confidence intervals or effect size are discussed and slight misinterpretation of p-value occurs. This provides some but not full support, placing it in the 3-4 band.

Means, standard deviations and a two-sample t-test are calculated; formulas are shown. There are calculation/notation errors (p-value written as 4Ã10^7) and no check of assumptions, so the analysis is correct in principle but incomplete, consistent with the 3-4 band.

Only one improvement (interactive workshops plus need for follow-up) is proposed; it partially addresses awareness outreach but does not tackle methodological weaknesses (unequal sample, survey design). Suggestions are therefore superficial to meaningful, aligning with the 1-2 band.

The student outlines an online Likert-scale survey, lists question categories, sets a target of â¥30 respondents per gender and describes basic statistical tests. These details allow another researcher to repeat the procedure, but the full question set, scoring rubric and exact survey link content are absent, so sufficiency of data collection cannot be guaranteed. This matches the mid-band descriptor.

Links between the environmental issue (aircraft noise) and the question (gender awareness) are described: measured noise exceeds WHO thresholds, gender may influence eco-behaviour, etc. Nonetheless, the explanation is fragmented and some causal reasoning (how awareness mitigates impact) is missing. This moves beyond a mere list but is not yet a comprehensive explanation, fitting the 3-4 band.

The research question is clearly identifiable and narrowly targets genderâbased differences in awareness of aircraft noise among a single student body. However, awkward phrasing (âto what extent are studentsâ¦ being awareâ) and the conflation of two foci (impact of routes and awareness) reduce precision. The question is therefore relevant and focused enough for the 3-4 band, but not polished or fully coherent to merit the top band.

Strengths (interactive, practical tips) and weaknesses (participation, resources, longevity) are noted. Not every relevant aspect (cost, scalability, policy constraints) is covered, but the evaluation goes beyond a list, fitting band 2.

The report follows a conventional IA structure with numbered sections and tables, yet formatting glitches, duplicated tables and LaTeX artefacts impede flow in places. Structure is present but not sustained flawlessly, meeting band 2.

Strengths (large sample, ease of surveys) and weaknesses (self-report bias, limited depth) are described, together with acknowledgement of location limitation. Limitations such as non-random response bias are missing, so the discussion is adequate but not exhaustive.

The background section offers a multi-paragraph discussion of noise pollution definitions, decibel thresholds, health impacts, local geography (distance to KLIA2, measured dB levels at the college) and gender literature. These details place the investigation firmly within an ESS context and explain why the issue matters at the study site. The depth satisfies the descriptor for a full discussion, though minor citation inaccuracies prevent awarding the absolute maximum of 6.

A stratified random (quota) approach by gender is explicitly justified as producing a more representative sample. The explanation of strata, equal chance, and minimum sample size (with citation) demonstrates clear justification, satisfying the top-band requirements; a minor weakness is that the final sample is unbalanced, hence score 5 not 6.

Ethical considerations (confidentiality, voluntary participation, no names/emails) and low physical risk are outlined, but no systematic risk table nor discussion of potential psychological stress is offered. The coverage is therefore an outline rather than a comprehensive description.

All questions are tabulated and bar charts for each item are provided. Although some figures are poorly labelled and tables mix raw data with LaTeX artefacts, the diagrams chosen are appropriate and convey the data set, matching the middle band.

The workshop idea is described with some rationale (hands-on engagement) linking back to findings that males are less aware. This provides a weak but existing justification, matching the 2-mark descriptor.

Arguments generally progress logically from data to inference, but inconsistencies in p-value notation and sudden topic shifts disturb coherence. Readers can follow the narrative with some effort, corresponding to band 2.

annotations.comment: The research question is relevant but phrasing is awkward and conflates focus; refine to clearly specify variables and scope (e.g., awareness of noise impact by gender).
The student identifies a focused, relevant research question directly linked to local noise pollution context, demonstrating clear alignment with the investigationâs aims.
The background provides a clear definition of noise pollution with credible WHO and National Geographic citations, establishing strong scientific context.
This paragraph lists noise sources extensively but lacks focus and structure; consider organizing causes under thematic subheadings to improve readability.
Break this dense paragraph into smaller sections and verify citation accuracy (e.g., Matthew Isaac reference) to strengthen clarity and academic rigour.
In the variables table, the dependent variable is mislabelled as an independent variable; ensure correct variable categorisation for method clarity.
The hypothesis is clear but could be more precise by explicitly defining the expected difference magnitude and linking back to the research question.
Include the full survey instrument or an appendix with question wording to ensure methodological repeatability and transparency.
Ethical considerations note confidentiality but omit potential psychological stress or bias in question framing; expand to include all risk categories.
The survey structure table clearly justifies each sectionâs purpose, demonstrating thoughtful design aligned with research objectives.
Justification for stratified random sampling is well articulated, showing clear alignment between sampling strategy and research variables.
Address the unequal final sample sizes by discussing potential impact on statistical power and representativeness.
The Likert-scale value table effectively presents scoring criteria, aiding clarity for subsequent data analyses.
While formulas for mean and standard deviation are correct, the write-up omits assumptions for t-tests and degrees of freedom; include these to strengthen analysis.
The mean calculation (434/31=14) oversimplifies decimals; provide full calculation with appropriate precision to avoid rounding ambiguity.
This large data table contains LaTeX artefacts (Î¸, overbars) and inconsistent formatting; clean symbols and simplify layout for reader comprehension.
Interpreting higher female participation as greater reliability overlooks response bias; address how participation rates may skew results.
Enhance figure captions by specifying chart type and variables (e.g., âBar chart of knowledge score by genderâ) to guide the reader effectively.
Add axis labels, units, and error bars to this bar chart to allow precise interpretation of differences between groups.
Include a legend and y-axis scale in the second bar chart to improve standalone interpretability without reference to text.
The worked example of percentage calculation clearly demonstrates the method, aiding transparency of data processing.
The scoring indicator table understates why multiple-option questions are excluded; clarify rationale to avoid misunderstanding of data inclusion.
The individual scores table is densely packed and difficult to read; consider summarising with key descriptive statistics or graphs.
Figure 1 effectively visualizes average practice scores by gender, clearly highlighting the observed difference.
P-value notation is incorrect (â4Ã10^7â); recheck calculation and use correct scientific notation (e.g., 4Ã10^â7) to avoid misinterpretation.
The p-value reported (4.00489Ã10^7) contradicts expected statistical results; verify the computation and correct order of magnitude.
The conclusion states causation (âinfluenced by genderâ) but data support correlation only; rephrase to reflect associative finding.
The method evaluation succinctly outlines strengths and weaknesses of the survey approach, demonstrating critical reflection.
Consider incorporating objective measures (e.g., noise frequency data, sensitivity scales) in future methods to address identified limitations.
The section title âSOLLUTIONâ contains a spelling error; ensure correct terminology (âSolutionâ) for professional presentation.
Link the workshop proposal to measurable outcomes and suggest how to evaluate its effectiveness for a more robust application plan.
Outline concrete metrics for assessing workshop impact (e.g., pre-/post-surveys, attendance rates) to strengthen the solution evaluation.
